title: Environment Variables
intro: Your app just crashed in production with 'command not found' even though the tool is installed. The problem isn't the tool itself -- it's that the system doesn't know where to look for it. Welcome to the world of environment variables and the mysterious PATH.

teach.title: Environment Variables = Sticky Notes on Your Monitor
teach.body: Imagine you have sticky notes around your monitor: one says 'DB password: abc123', another says 'API key: xyz789', and another says 'Deploy to: production'. Any time you need that info, you just glance at the note. **Environment variables are your system's sticky notes** -- they store key-value pairs that any program can read. Programs check these notes to know where the database is, which API key to use, and whether they're running in development or production.

teach.title: PATH = Your Phone's Contact List
teach.body: When you want to call someone, you don't type their full number every time -- you search your contacts by name and your phone knows the number. **The PATH variable is your system's contact list for programs.** When you type `node` or `python`, the system searches the directories listed in PATH to find that program. If the directory isn't in PATH, it's like having someone's number but not in your contacts -- you'd have to type the full path every time.

teach.title: What Are Environment Variables?
teach.body:
Environment variables are **key-value pairs** stored by your operating system:

- **`echo $PATH`** -- Shows the directories where the system looks for commands
- **`echo $HOME`** -- Shows your home directory
- **`export API_KEY="abc123"`** -- Sets a variable for the current session
- **`env`** -- Lists all current environment variables

In web development, `.env` files store project-specific variables like database URLs and API keys. Tools like dotenv load these into your app at runtime.

teach.title: Working with Environment Variables
teach.code:
# View the PATH variable
echo $PATH
# /usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin

# Set a variable for this session
export DATABASE_URL="postgres://localhost:5432/mydb"

# Use it in a command
echo $DATABASE_URL
# postgres://localhost:5432/mydb

# Add a directory to PATH
export PATH="$PATH:/usr/local/go/bin"
teach.annotation: Variables set with export only last for the current terminal session. To make them permanent, add the export line to your shell config file (~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, or similar).

teach.body: Environment variable issues cause some of the most confusing bugs in development. When you can diagnose 'command not found' or 'missing API key' in minutes, clients are impressed. That troubleshooting skill turns a $200 bug fix into a 15-minute vibecoding task at premium rates.

Environment variables store configuration that programs read at runtime. PATH tells the system where to find commands. Use export to set variables, echo $VAR to read them, and .env files for project-specific secrets. If a command isn't found, PATH is usually the culprit.

start.narrative:
INCIDENT: A developer reports that after installing a new Node.js version manager (nvm), their terminal shows 'node: command not found' even though Node was working fine yesterday. The app needs to be deployed within the hour.

You SSH into the server. What's your first move?

choices.text: Run 'echo $PATH' to check if the Node.js directory is in the PATH
Run 'which node' to see if the system can find the node binary

check-path.narrative:
You run `echo $PATH` and see:
/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin

The nvm directory isn't listed. Node was installed via nvm to ~/.nvm/versions/node/v20.11.0/bin/ but that path isn't in PATH. The system has no idea where to look for it. You've confirmed the diagnosis -- now move to a fix.

choices.text: Apply a temporary export to add the nvm bin directory to PATH
Skip the temp fix and edit the shell config file directly

which-node.narrative: You run `which node` and get no output -- the system can't find the node binary anywhere in the current PATH. The binary exists on disk (nvm installed it) but the PATH doesn't include its location. Now you know it's a PATH issue.

choices.text: Confirm with 'echo $PATH' to see exactly which directories are being searched
Skip the verification and start patching PATH directly

reinstall.narrative: You spend 20 minutes reinstalling Node.js, but after installation you get the same error: 'node: command not found.' Reinstalling didn't help because the problem was never about Node being missing -- it was about the system not knowing where Node lives.

choices.text: Stop and investigate PATH before installing anything else

temp-fix.narrative: You run `export PATH="$PATH:$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v20.11.0/bin"` and `node --version` finally works. The deploy script will run. But this export evaporates when the SSH session ends. What do you do before logging out?

choices.text: Open ~/.zshrc and confirm whether nvm's init lines are present and being sourced
Append the raw export line to ~/.zshrc as the permanent fix
Leave it -- the deploy is what matters and the temp fix held

permanent-fix.narrative:
You open ~/.zshrc and find the nvm init lines were stripped during a recent dotfile cleanup:

# (missing)
export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
[ -s "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh" ] && source "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh"

How do you restore them so future shells work cleanly?

choices.text: Add both lines back, then run `source ~/.zshrc` and open a fresh shell to confirm
Add the lines, save the file, and trust the next SSH session to pick them up
Hardcode the v20.11.0 bin path into PATH instead of sourcing nvm.sh

prevent-recurrence.narrative: The deploy goes through and `node --version` survives a fresh SSH session. But the dotfile cleanup that broke nvm could happen again, and other servers may have the same gap. What do you put in place to keep this from biting the team again?

choices.text: Pin the dotfiles in version control with a setup script that re-sources nvm, and add a healthcheck that runs `node --version` on deploy
Document the manual fix in the team wiki and move on
Nothing -- the immediate fire is out and the deploy succeeded

end-good.narrative: Dotfiles are now versioned. A deploy healthcheck runs `node --version` and aborts if PATH is broken. The next time someone cleans up shell config, the missing nvm lines fail loudly in CI instead of silently in production at 2am.
end-good.feedback: Excellent end-to-end response. You diagnosed PATH (not the install), made the fix permanent in shell config, and built a guardrail so the same dotfile drift can't silently take production down again. That's the difference between a one-time hero fix and a hardened system.

end-partial.narrative: The wiki page exists but nobody reads docs they didn't know they needed. The next time PATH breaks on a different server, someone burns another hour rediscovering the same nvm-init lines.
end-partial.feedback: Documentation is helpful but it's a passive guardrail -- it only protects people who already know to look. Active guardrails (versioned dotfiles, deploy healthchecks, CI smoke tests) catch this class of drift automatically. Pair the doc with one of those and the same incident won't recur.

end-bad.narrative: Two weeks later the staging server reboots, the SSH session opens fresh, nvm isn't sourced, and a teammate burns 90 minutes diagnosing the same 'command not found' error. The cycle repeats every time the dotfile cleanup happens.
end-bad.feedback: Walking away after the deploy succeeds means treating PATH as someone else's problem. Environment-variable bugs love to come back -- a fresh shell, a new server, a rebuild -- because nothing prevents the same dotfile drift from happening again. Always close the loop with a guardrail.
